text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a large uterus, starting by cutting the right round ligament with a harmonic ace device to increase the lateral mobility of the uterus.

The surgeon opens the posterior leaf of the broad ligament using the Clermont-Ferrand technique, increasing the distance between the uterus and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament. The IP ligament is controlled with bipolar current just below the ovary, allowing for safe cutting away from the right ureter.

The surgeon performs the same procedure on the left side, cutting the left round ligament and opening both the anterior and posterior leaf of the brow ligament. Traction is applied to the right side of the uterus, followed by pedicurization, coagulation, and cutting of the left infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament.

The surgeon begins the vesicovaginal dissection by cutting the anterior leaf of the brown ligament towards the bladder. Cranial traction is applied to cut the isthmic peritoneum, creating a visible space between the bladder and the cervical isthmic area.
The surgeon removes surrounding tissues on the right uterine pedicle, continuing the dissection toward the right uterosacral ligament to enhance uterine mobility.

The surgeon continues the reticulovaginal dissection by cutting the median part of the bladder pillar, freeing the bladder and revealing the anterior part of the uterine manipulator. The left uterine artery is controlled but not cut to decrease backflow on the opposite side.

The surgeon controls the right uterine pedicle using bipolar cautery over a two-centimeter area, followed by cutting the large artery with the Harmonic Ace, achieving a bloodless result. The dissection continues toward the right uterosacral ligament, and the same procedure is mirrored on the opposite side.

The surgeon cuts the previously coagulated left uterine vein and the cranial part of the left uterosacral ligament. Using a monopolar needle, the anterior, lateral, and right aspects of the vagina are incised in a bloodless manner. The monopolar needle is inserted into the left iliac trocar to cut the posterior aspect of the vagina.

The surgeon uses a Chardonnay knife, a 10mm cold blade device, to perform intra-abdominal hemissection of the uterus, which is now free from vaginal attachments. The uterus is divided into two parts for easier removal using a spooler ball and laparoscopic palsy forceps, with visible tracking of each section.

The surgeon begins vaginal closure using a 14mm needle with Vial Zero suture, placing the first stitch on the upper part of the left uterosacral ligament. Interrupted sutures are preferred, ensuring the entire vaginal mucosa is included to reduce post-operative bleeding risk. The intracorporeal knotting technique is used for securing the sutures.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the vaginal closure, ensuring both the right and left uterosacral ligaments are properly controlled. This marks the completion of the total laparoscopic hysterectomy for a large uterus.
